title: Suicide rate vs. income inequality
subtitle: The [Gini coefficient](#dod:gini) measures inequality on a scale from 0 to 1. Higher values indicate higher inequality. Suicide deaths are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.
note: Depending on the country and year, inequality data relates to income (measured after taxes and benefits) or to consumption, [per capita](#dod:per-capita). The suicide rate is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized).
caption:
Suicide rate vs. income inequality

The Gini coefficient measures inequality on a scale from 0 to 1. Higher values indicate higher inequality. Suicide deaths are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the Gini coefficient on the horizontal axis and the suicide rate per 100,000 people on the vertical axis, which is plotted on a logarithmic scale. The horizontal axis ranges from 0.25 to 0.55, while the vertical axis spans from 1 to over 20. Each circle represents a country, with its size corresponding to population, ranging from 600 million to 1.4 billion. The legend categorizes countries by continent using distinct colors: orange for North America, maroon for South America, purple for Africa, blue for Europe, teal for Asia, and green for Oceania. India and China appear as the largest circles due to their populations. Countries such as South Africa, Zambia, and Brazil are positioned toward the right side of the chart, indicating higher Gini coefficients above 0.5, while their suicide rates vary between approximately 5 and 20. In contrast, nations like Syria, Egypt, and Lebanon cluster in the lower-left area, with Gini coefficients below 0.35 and suicide rates under 1. Several European countries, including Poland, Moldova, and the Netherlands, group in the mid-range of the Gini scale around 0.3 to 0.35, with suicide rates between 10 and 20. The United States and Uruguay sit near a Gini coefficient of 0.4, with suicide rates around 15 to 20. The data includes age-standardized suicide rates, and inequality metrics relate to income or consumption per capita depending on the country and year.
